cards.content:
**Data Pipeline Components:**

1. **Ingestion** - Collecting data from sources
2. **Validation** - Checking data quality at entry
3. **Transformation** - Cleaning, normalizing, encoding
4. **Storage** - Persisting processed data
5. **Serving** - Making data available for training/inference

**Key Considerations:**
• Scalability for growing data volumes
• Latency requirements for real-time vs batch
• Error handling and recovery
• Monitoring and alerting
• Version control for reproducibility
• Security and access controls
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.2 - Tailoring the AI Data Collection and Preparation Phase

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is a baseline model?

options.text: The first model ever created
A simple reference model to compare against more complex approaches
The model at the base of a neural network
The model used in production

cards.explanation: A baseline model is a simple reference model (like random prediction or simple rules) used to establish minimum performance expectations. More complex models should demonstrably outperform the baseline to justify their complexity.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is regularization in machine learning?

options.text: Scheduling regular model updates
Techniques to prevent overfitting by adding constraints to the model
Standardizing data formats
Following regulatory requirements

cards.explanation: Regularization refers to techniques (like L1, L2, dropout) that add constraints or penalties to the model during training to prevent overfitting and improve generalization to new data.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is the purpose of a validation dataset?

options.text: To train the model
To tune hyperparameters and select the best model during development
To validate stakeholder requirements
To validate data quality

cards.explanation: A validation dataset is used during model development to tune hyperparameters and select the best model configuration. It's separate from training data (used to fit the model) and test data (used for final evaluation).

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is ensemble learning?

options.text: Training a team of data scientists together
Combining multiple models to improve overall prediction performance
Using multiple datasets for training
Running models in parallel for speed

cards.explanation: Ensemble learning combines predictions from multiple models (like random forests or boosting methods) to achieve better performance than any single model alone.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content:
**Train/Validation/Test Split:**

**Training Set (typically 60-80%)**
• Used to fit model parameters
• Model learns patterns from this data

**Validation Set (typically 10-20%)**
• Used during development
• Tune hyperparameters
• Select best model configuration
• Monitor for overfitting

**Test Set (typically 10-20%)**
• Held out until final evaluation
• Unbiased estimate of production performance
• Never used for model selection

**Key Principle:** Test data must remain unseen until final evaluation to provide an unbiased performance estimate.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.3 - Tailoring the AI Model Development Phase

cards.content: What is canary deployment?

options.text: Deploying AI for bird detection
Gradually releasing to a small subset of users before full rollout
Deploying with warning notifications
Testing in a yellow-labeled environment

cards.explanation: Canary deployment releases the new AI model to a small percentage of users first, allowing teams to monitor for issues before rolling out to the entire user base. If problems arise, only a small portion of users are affected.

cards.content: What is A/B testing in the context of AI deployment?

options.text: Testing between two development teams
Comparing two model versions by splitting user traffic between them
Testing models alphabetically
Alternating between two deployment schedules

cards.explanation: A/B testing splits user traffic between two versions (A and B) to compare their performance on business metrics. This enables data-driven decisions about which model version to deploy fully.

cards.content: What is a service level agreement (SLA) for AI systems?

options.text: A legal document for AI vendors
Commitments defining expected performance, availability, and response times
A software licensing agreement
An agreement between data scientists

cards.explanation: An SLA defines the expected performance characteristics of the AI system, including availability (uptime), response times (latency), throughput, and procedures for addressing failures.

cards.content: What is shadow mode deployment?

options.text: Deploying without documentation
Running a new model alongside production without affecting outputs
Deploying during off-hours
Hiding deployment from users

cards.explanation: Shadow mode runs the new AI model in parallel with the existing system. The new model processes real data and logs predictions, but its outputs don't affect production. This allows safe comparison before switching over.

cards.content:
**AI System Decommissioning Checklist:**

**Before Shutdown:**
• Notify all stakeholders and users
• Document final model performance
• Archive model artifacts and configurations
• Export relevant training data (if allowed)
• Transfer knowledge to replacement systems

**During Shutdown:**
• Graceful traffic migration
• Monitor for dependent systems
• Verify no critical processes affected

**After Shutdown:**
• Securely delete sensitive data
• Retain compliance documentation
• Document lessons learned
• Update system inventories
• Complete financial close-out
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.7 - Tailoring the AI End-of-Life and Decommissioning Phase

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is differential privacy?

options.text: Privacy policies that differ by region
A technique that adds noise to data to protect individual records while preserving aggregate patterns
Different privacy levels for different users
Comparing privacy policies between organizations

cards.explanation: Differential privacy is a mathematical framework that adds carefully calibrated noise to data or queries, ensuring that individual records cannot be identified while still allowing useful aggregate analysis.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is federated learning?

options.text: Learning across different government agencies
Training models across decentralized data sources without sharing raw data
A federal program for AI education
Combining models from different vendors

cards.explanation: Federated learning trains AI models across multiple decentralized devices or servers holding local data samples, without exchanging the raw data. This preserves privacy while enabling collaborative model training.

cards.content: What is technical debt in AI systems?

options.text: The cost of hardware
Accumulated maintenance burden from shortcuts or outdated approaches
Financial debt for technology purchases
Training costs for technical staff

cards.explanation: Technical debt refers to the accumulated maintenance burden resulting from shortcuts, quick fixes, or outdated approaches in AI systems. It includes things like undocumented code, deprecated dependencies, and configuration complexity.

cards.content: What is the purpose of stakeholder mapping in AI projects?

options.text: To create organization charts
To identify, analyze, and plan engagement strategies for all affected parties
To map geographical locations of stakeholders
To track stakeholder investments

cards.explanation: Stakeholder mapping identifies all parties affected by or interested in the AI project, analyzes their influence and interests, and helps plan appropriate engagement strategies for each group.

cards.content: What is data provenance?

options.text: The geographic origin of data
Documentation of data's origin, history, and transformations
Provincial data regulations
Proof of data ownership

cards.explanation: Data provenance documents the complete history of data including its origin, how it was collected, what transformations were applied, and who accessed it. This supports reproducibility, debugging, and compliance.

cards.content: What is a data dictionary?

options.text: A translation tool for multilingual data
Documentation defining data elements, formats, and meanings
An alphabetical list of data files
A database index

cards.explanation: A data dictionary is documentation that defines each data element including its name, description, data type, allowed values, relationships to other elements, and business meaning.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is transfer learning?

options.text: Moving models between servers
Applying a pre-trained model to a new but related task
Transferring skills between team members
Converting data between formats

cards.explanation: Transfer learning applies knowledge from a model trained on one task to a new, related task. This can significantly reduce training time and data requirements, especially for deep learning models.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is the confusion matrix used for?

options.text: To confuse adversarial attackers
To visualize classification model performance across all class predictions
To organize confusing requirements
To track team confusion about tasks

cards.explanation: A confusion matrix is a table showing the counts of true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for a classification model. It helps visualize where the model is making correct predictions versus errors.

cards.content: What is model latency?

options.text: How long a model remains in production
The time delay between receiving input and returning a prediction
The delay in model training
Time between model versions

cards.explanation: Model latency is the time delay between when the model receives an input and when it returns a prediction. Low latency is critical for real-time applications like fraud detection or recommendation systems.

cards.content: What is model throughput?

options.text: The model's accuracy rate
The number of predictions a model can process per unit of time
The amount of data passing through the model
The speed of model training

cards.explanation: Model throughput measures how many predictions or inferences a model can process per unit of time (e.g., predictions per second). High throughput is important for batch processing and high-volume applications.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content:
**Types of AI Bias:**

• **Selection Bias** - Training data not representative of population
• **Measurement Bias** - Systematic errors in data collection
• **Algorithmic Bias** - Model amplifies existing biases
• **Confirmation Bias** - Favoring information that confirms existing beliefs
• **Automation Bias** - Over-relying on automated decisions
• **Historical Bias** - Data reflects past discrimination
• **Representation Bias** - Underrepresentation of certain groups
• **Aggregation Bias** - Combining groups that should be separate

**Mitigation:** Diverse teams, bias audits, fairness metrics, inclusive data collection
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 2.5 - Principle of Ethics and Professional Responsibility

cards.content:
**AI Project Phases:**

1. **Discovery** - Problem identification, feasibility analysis
2. **Design** - Solution architecture, data requirements
3. **Development** - Data preparation, model training, testing
4. **Deployment** - Production release, integration, monitoring
5. **Operations** - Maintenance, retraining, optimization
6. **Decommission** - End-of-life planning, graceful shutdown

**Key Differences from Traditional Software:**
• Data dependency and quality requirements
• Experimental, iterative model development
• Performance degradation over time (drift)
• Continuous monitoring and retraining needs
• Unique ethical and regulatory considerations
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4 - Life Cycle Management and Tailoring Activities

cards.content:
**Data Preprocessing Techniques:**

**Cleaning:**
• Handle missing values (imputation, deletion)
• Remove duplicates
• Fix inconsistencies and errors
• Detect and handle outliers

**Transformation:**
• Normalization (0-1 scaling)
• Standardization (z-score)
• Log transformation for skewed data
• Encoding categorical variables

**Feature Engineering:**
• Create new features from existing ones
• Aggregate or combine features
• Extract temporal features
• Domain-specific transformations

**Documentation:** All transformations must be documented for reproducibility.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.2 - Tailoring the AI Data Collection and Preparation Phase

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content:
**Common ML Algorithm Categories:**

**Supervised Learning:**
• Classification: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, SVM, Neural Networks
• Regression: Linear Regression, Gradient Boosting, Neural Networks

**Unsupervised Learning:**
• Clustering: K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical
• Dimensionality Reduction: PCA, t-SNE

**Reinforcement Learning:**
• Q-Learning, Policy Gradients, Actor-Critic

**Deep Learning:**
• CNNs (images), RNNs/LSTMs (sequences), Transformers (text)

**Selection Criteria:** Problem type, data characteristics, interpretability needs, computational resources.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.3 - Tailoring the AI Model Development Phase

cards.content:
**Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) for ML:**

**CI (Continuous Integration):**
• Automated testing on code changes
• Data validation checks
• Model training on new data
• Performance regression tests

**CD (Continuous Deployment):**
• Automated model deployment pipelines
• Staged rollouts (canary, blue-green)
• Automated rollback on failures
• Configuration management

**ML-Specific Considerations:**
• Data versioning alongside code
• Model versioning and registry
• Experiment tracking integration
• Performance monitoring triggers
• A/B testing infrastructure
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 5.3.7 - Implementation

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is the EU AI Act's risk-based classification approach?

options.text: All AI systems are treated equally
AI systems are categorized by risk level with corresponding requirements
Only high-risk AI is regulated
Risk is determined by company size

cards.explanation: The EU AI Act classifies AI systems into risk categories (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal) with different regulatory requirements for each level. High-risk systems face the most stringent requirements.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is algorithmic accountability?

options.text: Algorithms that track expenses
Clear responsibility for AI system decisions and their consequences
Counting algorithm iterations
Auditing algorithm code

cards.explanation: Algorithmic accountability means establishing clear responsibility for AI system decisions and their consequences. It involves transparency about how decisions are made and who is responsible when things go wrong.

cards.content: What is a business sponsor in an AI project?

options.text: A company that provides funding
A senior executive accountable for the project's business outcomes
A sponsor of AI conferences
The project manager

cards.explanation: A business sponsor is a senior executive who champions the AI project, provides resources, removes organizational barriers, and is ultimately accountable for ensuring the project delivers business value.

cards.content: What distinguishes a program from a project in AI initiatives?

options.text: Programs are smaller than projects
Programs are groups of related projects managed together for greater benefits
Programs don't have deadlines
Programs are only for software development

cards.explanation: A program is a group of related projects and activities managed in a coordinated way to obtain benefits not available from managing them individually. AI programs often include multiple model development projects with shared infrastructure.

cards.content: What is data versioning?

options.text: Converting data between versions
Tracking and managing different versions of datasets over time
Numbering data files sequentially
Creating data backups

cards.explanation: Data versioning tracks and manages different versions of datasets over time, enabling reproducibility of experiments, rollback to previous states, and understanding how data changes affect model performance.

cards.content: What is a data lake?

options.text: A water feature in data centers
A storage repository holding vast amounts of raw data in native format
A small database
A data visualization tool

cards.explanation: A data lake is a centralized storage repository that holds vast amounts of raw data in its native format until needed. Unlike data warehouses, data lakes store data without predefined schema, enabling flexibility for AI/ML workloads.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is early stopping in model training?

options.text: Stopping a project early
Halting training when validation performance stops improving to prevent overfitting
Starting training early in the morning
Reducing training data size

cards.explanation: Early stopping monitors validation performance during training and stops when it begins to degrade, preventing the model from overfitting to training data while saving computational resources.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is a learning rate in model training?

options.text: How fast data scientists learn
A hyperparameter controlling how much to adjust the model per training step
The speed of training hardware
How fast users learn to use the model

cards.explanation: Learning rate is a hyperparameter that controls how much the model's parameters are adjusted during each training step. Too high causes instability; too low causes slow convergence or getting stuck.

cards.content: What is auto-scaling for AI services?

options.text: Automatically adjusting model weights
Automatically adjusting computational resources based on demand
Scaling data automatically
Automatic feature scaling

cards.explanation: Auto-scaling automatically adjusts computational resources (servers, containers) based on demand. This ensures the AI service can handle traffic spikes while minimizing costs during low-demand periods.

cards.content: What is a model serving infrastructure?

options.text: Customer service for AI products
The system that hosts and runs models to serve predictions in production
Training infrastructure
Data storage systems

cards.explanation: Model serving infrastructure hosts trained models and handles prediction requests in production. It includes components for load balancing, scaling, caching, and monitoring to ensure reliable model inference.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is the purpose of an AI ethics board or committee?

options.text: To approve AI budgets
To review AI initiatives for ethical implications and provide guidance
To manage AI development teams
To market AI products

cards.explanation: An AI ethics board reviews AI initiatives for ethical implications, provides guidance on responsible AI practices, and helps ensure AI systems align with organizational values and societal norms.

cards.content: What is a use case in AI project planning?

options.text: A storage container for AI models
A specific application scenario describing how users will interact with the AI system
A legal case study
A test case in software testing

cards.explanation: A use case describes a specific application scenario showing how users or systems will interact with the AI solution. It helps define requirements, validate solutions, and communicate with stakeholders.

cards.content: What is data anonymization?

options.text: Deleting all data
Removing or masking personally identifiable information from data
Making data anonymous to the team
Encrypting data

cards.explanation: Data anonymization removes or masks personally identifiable information (PII) from datasets so individuals cannot be identified. This enables data use for AI development while protecting privacy.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is batch normalization?

options.text: Processing data in batches
A technique that normalizes layer inputs to accelerate training and improve stability
Organizing batches of training jobs
Standardizing batch sizes

cards.explanation: Batch normalization normalizes the inputs of neural network layers during training, which helps accelerate training, allows higher learning rates, and can improve model generalization.

cards.content: What is a health check for AI services?

options.text: Medical examination for AI team members
Automated tests verifying the AI service is running correctly
Health insurance for AI projects
Wellness programs for data scientists

cards.explanation: Health checks are automated tests that verify an AI service is running correctly. They typically check system availability, response times, and basic functionality, enabling load balancers to route traffic only to healthy instances.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is the principle of data minimization?

options.text: Compressing data to save storage
Collecting only the data necessary for the specified purpose
Using the smallest models possible
Minimizing data costs

cards.explanation: Data minimization is a privacy principle requiring organizations to collect and retain only the data that is necessary for the specified purpose. It reduces privacy risks and is required by regulations like GDPR.

cards.content: What is a RACI matrix used for in AI projects?

options.text: To track model performance metrics
To clarify roles and responsibilities for tasks and decisions
To visualize data relationships
To schedule training runs

cards.explanation: A RACI matrix (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) clarifies who is responsible for each task, who has final authority, who should be consulted, and who should be kept informed throughout the project.

cards.content: What is feature selection?

options.text: Selecting product features to develop
Identifying the most relevant input variables for model training
Choosing software features
Selecting team members with specific features

cards.explanation: Feature selection identifies the most relevant input variables (features) for model training. It helps improve model performance, reduce overfitting, decrease training time, and improve interpretability.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is gradient descent?

options.text: A decline in model quality
An optimization algorithm that iteratively adjusts parameters to minimize loss
A downward trend in AI adoption
Reducing computational resources

cards.explanation: Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm that iteratively adjusts model parameters in the direction that reduces the loss function. It's the foundation of how most machine learning models learn from data.

cards.content: What is blue-green deployment?

options.text: Deploying to both development and production
Maintaining two identical environments and switching traffic between them
Using color-coded deployment scripts
Deploying during day and night shifts

cards.explanation: Blue-green deployment maintains two identical production environments (blue and green). New versions deploy to the inactive environment, and traffic is switched over once validated. This enables instant rollback by switching back.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What does 'right to explanation' mean in the context of AI?

options.text: The right to access AI source code
An individual's right to understand how an automated decision affecting them was made
The right to AI training
The right to challenge AI developers

cards.explanation: The right to explanation gives individuals the right to understand how automated decisions that significantly affect them were made. This is particularly important under regulations like GDPR and drives requirements for model explainability.

cards.content: What is a work breakdown structure (WBS) for AI projects?

options.text: A team organization chart
A hierarchical decomposition of project deliverables into manageable components
A budget breakdown
A risk analysis document

cards.explanation: A work breakdown structure hierarchically decomposes project deliverables into smaller, manageable work packages. For AI projects, this includes data work, model development, testing, deployment, and monitoring activities.

cards.content: What is data drift detection?

options.text: Tracking data migration between systems
Monitoring for changes in input data distributions that may affect model performance
Detecting unauthorized data access
Finding lost data files

cards.explanation: Data drift detection monitors for changes in the statistical properties of input data over time. When data drift is detected, it may signal that the model needs retraining to maintain performance.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is a loss function?

options.text: A function tracking financial losses
A mathematical function measuring the difference between predictions and actual values
A function for handling data loss
A business impact function

cards.explanation: A loss function (or cost function) measures the difference between model predictions and actual target values. The training process minimizes this function to improve model accuracy.

cards.content: What is observability in AI systems?

options.text: Making AI systems visible to users
The ability to understand internal system state from external outputs
Watching AI system demos
User interface visibility

cards.explanation: Observability is the ability to understand the internal state of a system by examining its external outputs (logs, metrics, traces). For AI systems, this includes model performance, data quality, and system health monitoring.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is model poisoning?

options.text: A corrupted model file
An attack where malicious data is injected during training to compromise model behavior
Toxic model outputs
Environmental contamination from AI systems

cards.explanation: Model poisoning is a security attack where adversaries inject malicious data into the training set to manipulate the model's learned behavior, potentially causing it to make incorrect predictions on specific inputs.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is meant by AI 'guardrails'?

options.text: Physical barriers around AI hardware
Constraints and safeguards to keep AI behavior within acceptable bounds
Security fences around data centers
Guidance for AI developers only

cards.explanation: AI guardrails are constraints, rules, and safeguards implemented to keep AI system behavior within acceptable bounds. They help prevent harmful outputs, enforce ethical guidelines, and maintain compliance.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content:
**AI Governance Framework Components:**

**Strategy & Policy:**
• AI ethics principles and values
• Acceptable use policies
• Risk appetite and tolerance

**Organization:**
• Roles and responsibilities (RACI)
• AI ethics board/committee
• Escalation procedures

**Processes:**
• AI project approval workflow
• Risk assessment procedures
• Monitoring and audit processes

**Technology:**
• Model registry and versioning
• Monitoring and alerting tools
• Documentation systems

**People:**
• Training and awareness
• Competency requirements
• Culture of responsibility
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 2.3 - Principle of Governance and Compliance

cards.content: What is an AI product roadmap?

options.text: A map of AI vendors
A strategic plan showing planned AI capabilities and their timeline
Directions to AI facilities
A visualization of AI algorithms

cards.explanation: An AI product roadmap is a strategic plan that outlines the planned AI capabilities, features, and improvements over time. It aligns AI development with business strategy and communicates priorities to stakeholders.

cards.content: What is the purpose of conducting a competitive analysis for AI solutions?

options.text: To copy competitor solutions
To understand market landscape and differentiate your AI solution
To find competitor weaknesses
To benchmark salaries

cards.explanation: Competitive analysis helps understand what AI solutions exist in the market, identify gaps and opportunities, and determine how to differentiate your solution. It informs strategy and positioning decisions.

cards.content: What is value stream mapping for AI?

options.text: Mapping revenue streams
Visualizing the end-to-end flow of activities to deliver AI value to customers
Streaming AI data
Mapping water resources

cards.explanation: Value stream mapping visualizes the end-to-end flow of activities required to deliver AI value to customers, identifying waste, bottlenecks, and opportunities for improvement in the AI delivery process.

cards.content:
**Build vs. Buy vs. Partner Decision Framework:**

**Build In-House When:**
• Core competitive differentiator
• Unique requirements not available commercially
• Need full control over IP and roadmap
• Have internal expertise

**Buy/License When:**
• Commoditized capability
• Time-to-market is critical
• Vendor has superior expertise
• Total cost of ownership is lower

**Partner When:**
• Need specialized expertise temporarily
• Risk sharing is valuable
• Complementary capabilities
• Learning opportunity

**Hybrid approaches** are common: buy platform, build differentiating layers.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 6.2 - AI in Portfolios, Programs, and Projects

cards.content: What is an ETL pipeline?

options.text: An Estimated Time to Launch calculation
A process to Extract, Transform, and Load data from sources to destinations
An External Team Link
An error tracking log

cards.explanation: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) is a data pipeline process that extracts data from source systems, transforms it into the required format, and loads it into a destination system for analysis or model training.

cards.content: What is a data warehouse?

options.text: A physical building storing servers
A structured repository optimized for analytical queries and reporting
A warehouse management system
A backup storage facility

cards.explanation: A data warehouse is a structured central repository optimized for analytical queries and reporting. Unlike data lakes, data warehouses enforce schema and structure, making them ideal for business intelligence.

cards.content: What is master data management (MDM)?

options.text: Management of master's degree programs
Processes ensuring consistent, accurate core business data across the organization
Managing database administrators
Primary data backup procedures

cards.explanation: Master data management establishes processes to ensure core business data (customers, products, locations) is consistent, accurate, and authoritative across all systems and applications.

cards.content:
**Data Access Patterns:**

**Batch Processing:**
• Process large volumes periodically
• Higher latency, higher throughput
• Cost-effective for historical analysis
• Example: Nightly model retraining

**Stream Processing:**
• Process data in real-time as it arrives
• Low latency, continuous
• Higher infrastructure complexity
• Example: Fraud detection

**Hybrid (Lambda Architecture):**
• Batch layer for historical data
• Speed layer for real-time updates
• Serving layer merges results

**Choice depends on:** Latency requirements, data volume, infrastructure maturity, cost constraints.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.2 - Tailoring the AI Data Collection and Preparation Phase

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is a neural network architecture?

options.text: Brain surgery procedures
The structure and organization of layers and connections in a neural network
Building designs for AI labs
Network infrastructure for AI

cards.explanation: Neural network architecture refers to how layers, neurons, and connections are organized in a neural network. Different architectures (CNN, RNN, Transformer) are suited for different types of problems.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is k-fold cross-validation?

options.text: Validating models k times
Dividing data into k parts and training k models, each using a different part for validation
Folding data k times
Cross-checking k team members

cards.explanation: K-fold cross-validation divides the dataset into k equal parts (folds). The model is trained k times, each time using a different fold for validation and the remaining folds for training, providing robust performance estimates.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is ROC-AUC?

options.text: Return on Capital - AI Usage Calculation
A metric measuring classification model performance across all thresholds
Rate of Change - Automated Update Cycle
Research on Computing - AI Usage Committee

cards.explanation: ROC-AUC (Receiver Operating Characteristic - Area Under Curve) measures how well a classification model distinguishes between classes across all possible classification thresholds. AUC of 1.0 is perfect; 0.5 is random.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is dropout in neural networks?

options.text: When team members leave the project
A regularization technique that randomly deactivates neurons during training
Removing failed experiments
Data loss during transfer

cards.explanation: Dropout is a regularization technique that randomly deactivates (sets to zero) a fraction of neurons during training. This prevents co-adaptation of neurons and helps reduce overfitting.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content:
**Model Complexity Trade-offs:**

**Simple Models (Linear Regression, Decision Trees):**
✅ Interpretable and explainable
✅ Fast training and inference
✅ Lower resource requirements
✅ Less prone to overfitting
❌ May underfit complex patterns

**Complex Models (Deep Neural Networks, Ensembles):**
✅ Can capture complex patterns
✅ Often higher accuracy
❌ Less interpretable (black box)
❌ More data and compute needed
❌ Prone to overfitting
❌ Harder to debug

**Rule of Thumb:** Start simple, add complexity only when justified by performance gains.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.3 - Tailoring the AI Model Development Phase

cards.content: What is containerization for AI deployment?

options.text: Shipping AI hardware in containers
Packaging AI applications with dependencies for consistent deployment anywhere
Containing AI within boundaries
Storage containers for model files

cards.explanation: Containerization (using Docker, Kubernetes) packages AI applications with all dependencies into portable containers that run consistently across different environments, simplifying deployment and scaling.

cards.content: What is an API endpoint for AI models?

options.text: The end of an API cable
A URL where clients can send requests and receive model predictions
An ending point for API development
A termination of AI services

cards.explanation: An API endpoint is a specific URL where clients can send requests (input data) and receive responses (model predictions). It's the interface through which applications interact with deployed AI models.

cards.content: What is edge deployment for AI?

options.text: Deploying at the edge of a network perimeter
Running AI models on devices close to data sources rather than in centralized servers
Cutting-edge deployment techniques
Deploying near geographic edges

cards.explanation: Edge deployment runs AI models on devices close to where data is generated (phones, IoT devices, local servers) rather than sending data to centralized cloud servers. This reduces latency and bandwidth while improving privacy.

cards.content: What is model compression?

options.text: Zipping model files
Techniques to reduce model size while maintaining performance
Compressing training time
Reducing team size

cards.explanation: Model compression techniques (pruning, quantization, distillation) reduce model size and computational requirements while maintaining acceptable performance. This enables deployment on resource-constrained devices.

cards.content:
**Incident Management for AI Systems:**

**Detection:**
• Automated monitoring alerts
• User reports
• Performance degradation signals

**Triage:**
• Severity classification (P1-P4)
• Impact assessment
• Initial diagnosis

**Response:**
• Immediate mitigation (rollback, disable)
• Root cause investigation
• Communication to stakeholders

**Resolution:**
• Fix implementation
• Validation and deployment
• Documentation

**Post-Incident:**
• Blameless post-mortem
• Lessons learned
• Preventive measures
• Process improvements
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 5.3.8 - Operations and Maintenance

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is a data protection impact assessment (DPIA)?

options.text: An assessment of data backup systems
A process to identify and minimize privacy risks of data processing activities
An evaluation of data center security
A test of data encryption strength

cards.explanation: A DPIA is a process to systematically identify and minimize privacy risks of data processing activities. Required under GDPR for high-risk processing, it helps ensure AI projects comply with privacy regulations.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is meant by 'AI safety'?

options.text: Physical safety of AI hardware
Ensuring AI systems behave as intended without causing harm
Safety training for AI developers
Fire safety in data centers

cards.explanation: AI safety is the field concerned with ensuring AI systems behave as intended, remain under human control, and do not cause unintended harm. It encompasses alignment, robustness, and fail-safe mechanisms.

cards.content: What is a pilot program in AI deployment?

options.text: Training AI to pilot aircraft
A limited deployment to test the AI solution before full rollout
A program for pilot staff
The first program in a series

cards.explanation: A pilot program is a limited, controlled deployment of an AI solution with a subset of users or use cases. It validates the solution works as expected and gathers feedback before broader rollout.

cards.content: What is technical viability in AI feasibility assessment?

options.text: Whether the AI vendor is viable
Whether the proposed AI solution can be built with available technology and resources
The lifespan of AI technology
Technical team availability

cards.explanation: Technical viability assesses whether the proposed AI solution can actually be built given available technology, data, computational resources, expertise, and constraints. It's a key component of feasibility analysis.

cards.content: What is one-hot encoding?

options.text: Encoding data once
Converting categorical variables into binary vectors
Encryption at high temperatures
A single encoding attempt

cards.explanation: One-hot encoding converts categorical variables into binary vectors where each category becomes a column with 1 indicating presence and 0 indicating absence. It enables machine learning algorithms to process categorical data.

cards.content: What is data imputation?

options.text: Accusing data of being incorrect
Filling in missing values with estimated or calculated values
Importing data from external sources
Computing data statistics

cards.explanation: Data imputation fills in missing values using techniques like mean/median substitution, interpolation, or predictive modeling. This preserves dataset size while handling incomplete records.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is an activation function?

options.text: A function that activates the AI system
A mathematical function that determines if a neuron should be activated
A startup function for models
A function to activate team members

cards.explanation: An activation function in neural networks determines whether a neuron should be activated based on its inputs. Common functions include ReLU, sigmoid, and tanh, each with different properties.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is precision in classification metrics?

options.text: How precisely the model is built
The proportion of positive predictions that are actually correct
Decimal places in predictions
Precision of training data

cards.explanation: Precision measures the proportion of positive predictions that are actually correct (True Positives / (True Positives + False Positives)). High precision means few false positives.

cards.content: What is a feature flag for AI models?

options.text: A flag marking important features
A mechanism to enable or disable AI features dynamically without redeployment
A feature request system
A flag indicating feature completion

cards.explanation: Feature flags allow teams to enable or disable AI features dynamically without code redeployment. This enables gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and quick rollback of problematic features.

cards.content: What is load balancing for AI services?

options.text: Balancing work among team members
Distributing incoming requests across multiple model instances
Balancing data loads
Loading balance from financial accounts

cards.explanation: Load balancing distributes incoming prediction requests across multiple model instances to ensure no single instance is overwhelmed. This improves reliability, availability, and response times.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content:
**Responsible AI Practices Checklist:**

**Design Phase:**
☐ Diverse team composition
☐ Ethical review of use case
☐ Bias risk assessment
☐ Privacy by design

**Development Phase:**
☐ Representative training data
☐ Bias detection and mitigation
☐ Explainability mechanisms
☐ Security testing

**Deployment Phase:**
☐ Human oversight procedures
☐ Appeal/correction mechanisms
☐ Monitoring for drift and bias
☐ Incident response plan

**Operations Phase:**
☐ Ongoing fairness audits
☐ User feedback collection
☐ Regular model reviews
☐ Compliance monitoring
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 7 - Ethical and Legal Considerations

cards.content:
**AI Adoption Maturity Levels:**

**Level 1 - Exploring:**
• POCs and experiments
• Limited production use
• Siloed efforts

**Level 2 - Implementing:**
• Some production models
• Basic MLOps practices
• Growing expertise

**Level 3 - Scaling:**
• Multiple models in production
• Standardized processes
• Platform investments

**Level 4 - Optimizing:**
• AI embedded in operations
• Advanced automation
• Continuous improvement

**Level 5 - Transforming:**
• AI-first strategy
• Innovation leadership
• New business models enabled by AI

**Assessment informs** strategy, investment, and capability building priorities.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 5.1 - Portfolio Management and Artificial Intelligence

cards.content:
**Handling Missing Data:**

**Deletion Methods:**
• Listwise deletion (remove entire record)
• Pairwise deletion (use available data per analysis)
• Risk: Loss of information, potential bias

**Imputation Methods:**
• Mean/median/mode substitution
• Regression imputation
• K-nearest neighbors
• Multiple imputation
• Model-based (MICE)

**When to Use What:**
• < 5% missing: Simple imputation usually fine
• 5-30%: Consider multiple imputation
• > 30%: May need to exclude feature or collect more data

**Always:** Document missing data handling and assess impact.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.2 - Tailoring the AI Data Collection and Preparation Phase

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content:
**Experiment Tracking Best Practices:**

**What to Track:**
• Hyperparameters
• Model architecture
• Training data version
• Preprocessing steps
• Metrics (train, validation, test)
• Training duration
• Resource usage

**Tools:** MLflow, Weights & Biases, Neptune, TensorBoard

**Benefits:**
• Reproducibility of results
• Easy comparison of experiments
• Collaboration across team
• Audit trail for compliance
• Knowledge preservation

**Best Practice:** Start tracking from day one; retrofitting is painful.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.3 - Tailoring the AI Model Development Phase

cards.content:
**Model Retraining Triggers:**

**Performance-Based:**
• Accuracy drops below threshold
• Prediction distribution changes
• Business KPI degradation

**Data-Based:**
• Data drift detected
• Concept drift detected
• New data becomes available

**Time-Based:**
• Scheduled intervals (daily/weekly/monthly)
• Regulatory requirements

**Event-Based:**
• Business rule changes
• Feature availability changes
• External factors (market shifts)

**Best Practice:** Combine multiple triggers; don't rely on schedule alone.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.6 - Tailoring the AI Optimization and Iteration Phase

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What does 'purpose limitation' mean in data protection?

options.text: Limiting the purpose of AI projects
Data should only be used for the purposes for which it was collected
Limiting the number of AI use cases
Restricting team purposes

cards.explanation: Purpose limitation is a data protection principle requiring that personal data only be used for the specific, explicit purposes for which it was collected. Using data for new purposes requires additional consent or legal basis.

cards.content: What is opportunity cost in AI investment decisions?

options.text: The cost of opportunities
The value of the next best alternative foregone when choosing an investment
Costs for finding opportunities
Investment in opportunity discovery

cards.explanation: Opportunity cost represents the value of the next best alternative that must be foregone when choosing a particular investment. In AI decisions, it helps compare the value of different potential AI projects.

cards.content: What is data cataloging?

options.text: Ordering data catalogs
Creating an organized inventory of data assets with metadata
Categorizing shopping catalogs
Cataloging team data skills

cards.explanation: Data cataloging creates an organized inventory of data assets with metadata including descriptions, ownership, lineage, quality metrics, and access information. It helps users discover and understand available data.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is recall in classification metrics?

options.text: Remembering previous experiments
The proportion of actual positives that were correctly identified
Recalling deployed models
Memory recall in neural networks

cards.explanation: Recall (sensitivity) measures the proportion of actual positives that were correctly identified (True Positives / (True Positives + False Negatives)). High recall means few false negatives.

cards.content: What is circuit breaker pattern in AI services?

options.text: Electrical safety for AI hardware
A pattern that stops requests to a failing service to prevent cascade failures
Breaking model training circuits
Disconnecting faulty servers

cards.explanation: The circuit breaker pattern stops sending requests to a failing service after a threshold of failures, preventing cascade failures and giving the service time to recover. It's essential for resilient AI systems.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is informed consent in the context of AI data collection?

options.text: Consent from informed experts
Agreement given after being fully informed about how data will be used
Information consent forms
Consent to receive information

cards.explanation: Informed consent means individuals agree to data collection and use after being fully informed about what data is collected, how it will be used, who will access it, and their rights regarding the data.

cards.content: What is scope creep in AI projects?

options.text: Expanding the project scope without proper controls or approvals
Scope that moves slowly
Creeping up on project scope
Scope that creeps into other projects

cards.explanation: Scope creep refers to uncontrolled expansion of project scope without proper change control, approvals, or adjustments to timeline and resources. AI projects are particularly susceptible due to their experimental nature.

cards.content: What is data stratification?

options.text: Layering data storage
Dividing data into distinct groups based on specific characteristics
Strategic data planning
Data strength testing

cards.explanation: Data stratification divides data into distinct subgroups (strata) based on specific characteristics. This ensures representative sampling and enables analysis of performance across different segments.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is the F1 score?

options.text: Formula One racing score
The harmonic mean of precision and recall
First function score
Feature importance score

cards.explanation: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single metric that balances both. It's particularly useful when you need to balance false positives and false negatives.

cards.content: What is graceful degradation in AI systems?

options.text: Politely shutting down systems
Maintaining partial functionality when components fail rather than complete failure
Gradual model quality decline
Elegant system transitions

cards.explanation: Graceful degradation ensures an AI system maintains partial functionality when components fail rather than completely failing. For example, falling back to a simpler model or cached results when the primary model is unavailable.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is data sovereignty?

options.text: Royal ownership of data
Legal concept that data is subject to the laws of the nation where it resides
Supreme data authority
Data independence

cards.explanation: Data sovereignty is the concept that data is subject to the laws and governance of the nation where it is collected or stored. This affects where AI training data can be processed and stored.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is the principle of proportionality in AI data use?

options.text: Using proportional fonts in documentation
Data processing should be proportionate to the legitimate purpose
Proportional team sizes
Proportional resource allocation

cards.explanation: Proportionality means the extent of data processing should be proportionate to the legitimate purpose being pursued. Excessive data collection or processing beyond what's needed violates this principle.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is an AI impact assessment?

options.text: Assessment of AI market impact
A systematic evaluation of AI system effects on rights, safety, and society
Impact of AI on budgets
Physical impact testing of AI hardware

cards.explanation: An AI impact assessment systematically evaluates the potential effects of an AI system on human rights, safety, ethics, and society. It helps identify and mitigate negative impacts before deployment.

cards.content: What is a critical success factor (CSF) for AI projects?

options.text: A factor causing project criticism
An essential element that must be achieved for the project to succeed
A critical team member
A success rate calculation

cards.explanation: Critical success factors are essential elements that must be present or achieved for an AI project to succeed. Examples include data quality, stakeholder buy-in, and appropriate expertise.

cards.content: What is the purpose of a project charter for AI initiatives?

options.text: To charter flights for team travel
To formally authorize the project and document objectives, scope, and stakeholders
To chart project timelines
To create organizational charts

cards.explanation: A project charter formally authorizes the AI project and documents its objectives, scope, stakeholders, high-level requirements, and the authority granted to the project manager.

cards.content: What is net present value (NPV) in AI investment analysis?

options.text: Network performance value
The present value of future cash flows minus the initial investment
Current value of AI systems
Net project variance

cards.explanation: NPV calculates the present value of expected future cash flows from an AI investment, minus the initial investment cost. Positive NPV indicates the investment is expected to create value.

cards.content: What is data deduplication?

options.text: Doubling data for backup
Identifying and removing duplicate records from a dataset
Duplicating important data
Dedicating data to specific uses

cards.explanation: Data deduplication identifies and removes duplicate records from datasets. This improves data quality, reduces storage costs, and prevents models from being biased by overrepresented records.

cards.content: What is referential integrity in data management?

options.text: Integrity of reference documents
Ensuring relationships between related data records remain consistent
References in research papers
Referring data to the correct team

cards.explanation: Referential integrity ensures that relationships between related records remain consistent. For example, if a customer record is deleted, related order records should be handled appropriately.

cards.content: What is the purpose of data profiling?

options.text: Creating profiles of data users
Analyzing data to understand its structure, content, and quality
Profiling data center performance
Creating data portraits

cards.explanation: Data profiling analyzes datasets to understand their structure, content, relationships, and quality issues. It's an essential first step in data preparation for AI projects.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is stratified sampling?

options.text: Sampling from strategic sources
Sampling that maintains the proportion of subgroups from the original population
Layered data collection
Strategic sampling plans

cards.explanation: Stratified sampling ensures that the sample maintains the same proportions of important subgroups as the original population. This is crucial for ensuring representative training and test sets.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is the vanishing gradient problem?

options.text: Gradients that disappear from storage
When gradients become too small for effective learning in deep networks
Gradients that vanish after training
Missing gradient calculations

cards.explanation: The vanishing gradient problem occurs in deep neural networks when gradients become extremely small as they propagate backward, making early layers learn very slowly or not at all.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is ablation study in model development?

options.text: Studying model removal
Systematically removing components to understand their contribution
Ablating training data
Medical studies for AI

cards.explanation: An ablation study systematically removes or disables model components (features, layers, modules) to understand their contribution to overall performance. It helps identify which components are most valuable.

cards.content: What is model quantization?

options.text: Counting model parameters
Reducing numerical precision of model weights to decrease size and increase speed
Quantum computing for models
Quantifying model quality

cards.explanation: Model quantization reduces the numerical precision of model weights (e.g., from 32-bit to 8-bit) to decrease model size and increase inference speed, often with minimal accuracy loss.

cards.content: What is model pruning?

options.text: Trimming model documentation
Removing unnecessary parameters or connections to reduce model size
Pruning the model garden
Removing outdated models

cards.explanation: Model pruning removes unnecessary or redundant parameters, weights, or connections from a neural network to reduce model size and computational requirements while maintaining acceptable performance.

cards.content: What is model distillation?

options.text: Extracting model essence
Training a smaller model to mimic a larger, more complex model
Distilling data for models
Chemical processing of model data

cards.explanation: Model distillation (knowledge distillation) trains a smaller 'student' model to mimic the behavior of a larger 'teacher' model, creating a more efficient model that retains much of the original's capability.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content:
**AI Audit Components:**

**Technical Audit:**
• Model performance metrics
• Data quality assessment
• Security vulnerability scan
• Code review

**Ethical Audit:**
• Bias testing results
• Fairness metrics
• Privacy compliance
• Transparency assessment

**Governance Audit:**
• Policy compliance
• Documentation completeness
• Access control review
• Change management adherence

**Operational Audit:**
• Monitoring effectiveness
• Incident response capability
• Backup and recovery
• SLA compliance

**Frequency:** Risk-based; high-risk AI may require annual or more frequent audits.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 2.3.7 - Performance Monitoring and Metrics

cards.content:
**Stakeholder Analysis for AI Projects:**

**Identify:**
• Project sponsors and champions
• End users and affected parties
• Technical teams (data, ML, ops)
• Compliance and legal
• External partners and vendors

**Analyze:**
• Influence level (high/medium/low)
• Interest level (supporter/neutral/opponent)
• Information needs
• Potential concerns

**Strategy:**
• High influence + high interest: Manage closely
• High influence + low interest: Keep satisfied
• Low influence + high interest: Keep informed
• Low influence + low interest: Monitor

**Engagement:** Tailor communication and involvement based on analysis.
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 3.1 - Managing Stakeholder Expectations About AI

cards.content:
**Data Security for AI:**

**At Rest:**
• Encryption (AES-256)
• Access controls (RBAC)
• Secure storage configuration
• Key management

**In Transit:**
• TLS/SSL encryption
• Secure protocols (HTTPS, SFTP)
• VPN for sensitive transfers

**In Use:**
• Secure compute environments
• Memory protection
• Confidential computing

**Special AI Considerations:**
• Model as sensitive IP
• Training data protection
• Inference data handling
• Federated learning options
• Differential privacy techniques
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 3.3.4 - Security, Privacy, and Risk Management

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content:
**Classification Metrics Summary:**

**Accuracy:** (TP+TN)/(TP+TN+FP+FN)
• Overall correctness
• Can be misleading with imbalanced data

**Precision:** TP/(TP+FP)
• Of predicted positives, how many are correct?
• Important when false positives are costly

**Recall (Sensitivity):** TP/(TP+FN)
• Of actual positives, how many detected?
• Important when false negatives are costly

**F1 Score:** 2*(Precision*Recall)/(Precision+Recall)
• Harmonic mean balancing precision/recall

**Specificity:** TN/(TN+FP)
• True negative rate

TP=True Positive, TN=True Negative, FP=False Positive, FN=False Negative
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 3.3.5 - Monitoring, Evaluating, and Validating

cards.content:
**AI Production Checklist:**

**Pre-Deployment:**
☐ Model approved by stakeholders
☐ Performance meets acceptance criteria
☐ Security review completed
☐ Documentation complete
☐ Rollback plan tested

**Deployment:**
☐ Staged rollout (canary/blue-green)
☐ Monitoring dashboards active
☐ Alerting configured
☐ Logging enabled
☐ Health checks passing

**Post-Deployment:**
☐ Performance validated in production
☐ No errors or anomalies
☐ User feedback positive
☐ Runbook handed to operations
☐ Knowledge transfer complete
cards.explanation: Source: PMI Standard for AI in PPPM, Section 4.3.4 - Tailoring the AI Deployment Phase

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is a red team exercise for AI?

options.text: A team-building exercise
Adversarial testing where a team tries to find flaws and vulnerabilities
An emergency response drill
A competitive coding challenge

cards.explanation: Red team exercises involve a dedicated team attempting to find flaws, vulnerabilities, and failure modes in AI systems through adversarial testing. This helps identify risks before malicious actors do.

cards.content: What is a lessons learned session for AI projects?

options.text: Training sessions for team members
A structured review capturing what worked, what didn't, and improvements
Learning AI techniques
Reviewing educational materials

cards.explanation: Lessons learned sessions are structured reviews conducted after project milestones or completion to capture what worked well, what didn't, and recommendations for improvement in future projects.

cards.content: What is a data quality scorecard?

options.text: Scoring data scientists
A tool measuring data quality across multiple dimensions with scores
A scorecard for data games
Quality ratings for data vendors

cards.explanation: A data quality scorecard measures data quality across multiple dimensions (accuracy, completeness, timeliness, etc.) with quantitative scores, enabling monitoring and improvement over time.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is a holdout set?

options.text: Data held by a vendor
A portion of data kept separate from training for final evaluation
Data on hold status
Reserved storage capacity

cards.explanation: A holdout set is a portion of data kept completely separate from training and validation, used only for final model evaluation to get an unbiased estimate of production performance.

cards.content: What is chaos engineering for AI systems?

options.text: Disorganized engineering practices
Deliberately introducing failures to test system resilience
Chaotic model training
Random development approaches

cards.explanation: Chaos engineering deliberately introduces failures (network issues, service outages) in controlled ways to test how AI systems respond and recover, building confidence in system resilience.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)?

options.text: A model architecture
A method to explain individual predictions by showing feature contributions
A training technique
A data format

cards.explanation: SHAP is an explainability method that uses game theory (Shapley values) to show how each feature contributes to individual predictions, providing interpretable explanations for model decisions.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations)?

options.text: A citrus-flavored AI
A technique explaining predictions by approximating the model locally
A data visualization tool
A training framework

cards.explanation: LIME explains individual predictions by creating a simpler, interpretable model that approximates the complex model's behavior locally around the prediction point, showing which features influenced the decision.

cards.content: What is a center of excellence (CoE) for AI?

options.text: A physical building for AI
A centralized team providing AI expertise, standards, and best practices
An award for AI excellence
The best AI model

cards.explanation: An AI Center of Excellence is a centralized team that provides AI expertise, establishes standards and best practices, supports project teams, and drives AI capability development across the organization.

cards.content: What is target leakage in training data?

options.text: Data leaving the target system
When training data contains information that wouldn't be available at prediction time
Leaking target variable values
Target audience information leakage

cards.explanation: Target leakage occurs when training data includes information that wouldn't be available when making predictions in production. This leads to overly optimistic training metrics that don't translate to real-world performance.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is a learning curve in ML?

options.text: A path for learning ML
A plot showing model performance vs. training data size or iterations
Difficulty of learning algorithms
Team learning progress

cards.explanation: A learning curve plots model performance against training data size or training iterations. It helps diagnose overfitting/underfitting and estimate how much more data might improve performance.

cards.content: What is inference optimization?

options.text: Optimizing logical inference
Techniques to make model predictions faster and more efficient
Optimizing inference documentation
Statistical inference improvements

cards.explanation: Inference optimization includes techniques (batching, caching, hardware acceleration, model optimization) to make model predictions faster and more efficient in production environments.

cards.content: What is time-to-value in AI projects?

options.text: Time spent valuing assets
The duration from project start to when business value is realized
Valuable time management
Time tracking for value billing

cards.explanation: Time-to-value measures the duration from when an AI project begins to when it starts delivering measurable business value. Shorter time-to-value is often a key success factor for AI investments.

cards.content: What is data freshness?

options.text: How recently data was backed up
How current and up-to-date the data is for the intended use
Data storage temperature
New data arrivals

cards.explanation: Data freshness refers to how current and up-to-date data is relative to the real-world state it represents. Stale data can lead to model predictions that don't reflect current conditions.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is class imbalance?

options.text: Unequal classroom sizes
When classes in the target variable have significantly different frequencies
Imbalanced class schedules
Unequal class participation

cards.explanation: Class imbalance occurs when classes in the target variable have significantly different frequencies (e.g., 99% negative, 1% positive). This can bias models toward majority classes and requires special handling techniques.

cards.content: What is warm start vs. cold start for models?

options.text: Temperature-based model operation
Whether a model instance is already loaded and ready vs. needs initialization
Warm weather vs. cold weather deployment
Starting training from scratch vs. pretrained

cards.explanation: A warm start means a model instance is already loaded and ready to serve predictions immediately. A cold start requires loading the model first, causing latency. This affects user experience and scaling strategies.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is counterfactual explanation in AI?

options.text: Explaining counter-terrorism AI
Showing what input changes would lead to a different prediction
Factual vs. fictional explanations
Counting explanation factors

cards.explanation: Counterfactual explanations show what changes to inputs would result in a different prediction (e.g., 'Your loan would be approved if your income were $5,000 higher'). They provide actionable insight for affected individuals.

cards.content: What is agile methodology in AI development?

options.text: Quick physical movements
An iterative approach emphasizing flexibility, collaboration, and incremental delivery
Fast algorithm execution
Agility testing for AI systems

cards.explanation: Agile methodology in AI development emphasizes iterative development, flexibility to adapt based on learnings, collaboration between teams, and incremental delivery of value through working AI capabilities.

cards.content: What is entity resolution?

options.text: Resolving business entity structures
Identifying when different records refer to the same real-world entity
Entity relationship diagrams
Legal entity registration

cards.explanation: Entity resolution identifies when different records (possibly from different sources) refer to the same real-world entity (person, company, product). This is crucial for data integration and avoiding duplicates.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique)?

options.text: A smoking detection model
A technique creating synthetic samples to balance imbalanced datasets
A smooth training technique
Small model optimization

cards.explanation: SMOTE creates synthetic samples of minority class examples by interpolating between existing minority samples. This helps address class imbalance without simply duplicating existing records.

cards.content: What is GPU acceleration for AI inference?

options.text: Speeding up GPU manufacturing
Using graphics processing units to speed up model predictions
GPU display acceleration
Accelerating GPU procurement

cards.explanation: GPU acceleration uses graphics processing units to speed up model inference through parallel processing. GPUs can process many operations simultaneously, significantly reducing prediction latency for appropriate workloads.

cards.domain_name: Support Responsible and Trustworthy AI Efforts
cards.content: What is model card documentation?

options.text: Business cards for model developers
Standardized documentation of model details, intended use, and limitations
Credit card integration for models
Model identification cards

cards.explanation: Model cards are standardized documentation that includes model details (type, training data, performance), intended use cases, limitations, ethical considerations, and factors that may affect performance.

cards.content: What is a sprint in agile AI development?

options.text: Running quickly
A fixed time period for completing a set of planned work
Sprint phone carrier integration
Fast model training

cards.explanation: A sprint is a fixed time period (typically 1-4 weeks) during which a team completes a set of planned work items. At the end, working functionality is demonstrated and progress is reviewed.

cards.content: What is data masking?

options.text: Wearing masks while handling data
Replacing sensitive data with realistic but fictional values
Hiding data from view
Mask-wearing detection in AI

cards.explanation: Data masking replaces sensitive data with realistic but fictional values to protect privacy while maintaining data utility for development and testing purposes.

cards.domain_name: Manage AI Model Development and Evaluation
cards.content: What is a pipeline in ML development?

options.text: Oil and gas infrastructure
An automated sequence of data processing and model training steps
A communication channel
Water pipeline systems

cards.explanation: An ML pipeline is an automated sequence of steps for data processing, feature engineering, model training, and evaluation. Pipelines ensure reproducibility and enable continuous training workflows.

cards.content: What is a dead letter queue for AI services?

options.text: A queue for undeliverable mail
A queue storing failed requests for later analysis and retry
Outdated letters about AI
A queue for expired messages

cards.explanation: A dead letter queue stores messages or requests that couldn't be processed successfully. For AI services, this enables analysis of failures, debugging, and potential retry of failed predictions.
